title: Listening - Part 3: Choosing a Bicycle
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about why they chose a particular bicycle. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for their choice. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about why they chose a particular bicycle. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for their choice. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about choosing a new bicycle.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I moved to a flat on the third floor, and there isn't a secure place to leave bicycles outside. My old bike was far too heavy to take up the stairs, and the hallway indoors is extremely narrow. [19]I bought a folding bicycle because I can carry it upstairs and keep it behind the front door without it blocking the space or annoying my flatmates.[/19] It isn't as fast as my previous one, but at least I no longer worry about it disappearing overnight.||Speaker 2 Man: I don’t own a car and do most weekly errands by bike. Previously, I tried hanging shopping bags from the handlebars, which was uncomfortable and once caused me to lose my balance. [20]The bicycle I chose has a large fixed basket at the front and strong bags over the back wheel, so I can transport groceries safely instead of struggling with them.[/20] It's heavier than a sports bike, but I didn’t buy it for speed; I bought it to make ordinary shopping trips practical.||Speaker 3 Woman: Most of my cycling is on woodland trails near where I live. They're great fun, but in winter there are muddy areas, stones and tree roots everywhere. The light city bike I borrowed from my sister clearly wasn't designed for that. [21]I selected a mountain bike with wide tyres and strong suspension because it can handle rough paths without slipping or making every bump painful.[/21] It's less convenient in town, but on the routes I actually enjoy riding, it has made an enormous difference.||Speaker 4 Man: My aim this year is to cycle to the coast, which is over sixty kilometres from home. The bike I had at school was fine for short rides, but my shoulders ached after half an hour on it. [22]At the shop I tried several models and chose the one with a riding position and seat that felt comfortable enough for spending several hours on the road.[/22] I paid slightly more than intended, but during training rides I’ve had no discomfort, so it was worthwhile.||Speaker 5 Woman: I was saving for a bicycle to use at university, although I expected to wait until summer before I had enough money. Then a sports shop near us announced it was closing. [23]The model I wanted was being sold at almost half its normal price in the final sale, so I bought it immediately rather than risk losing such a good bargain.[/23] The colour wouldn't have been my first choice, but when something costs that much less, I can live with bright orange.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I moved to a flat on the third floor, and there isn't a secure place to leave bicycles outside. My old bike was far too heavy to take up the stairs, and the hallway indoors is extremely narrow. I bought a folding bicycle because I can carry it upstairs and keep it behind the front door without it blocking the space or annoying my flatmates. It isn't as fast as my previous one, but at least I no longer worry about it disappearing overnight.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I don’t own a car and do most weekly errands by bike. Previously, I tried hanging shopping bags from the handlebars, which was uncomfortable and once caused me to lose my balance. The bicycle I chose has a large fixed basket at the front and strong bags over the back wheel, so I can transport groceries safely instead of struggling with them. It's heavier than a sports bike, but I didn’t buy it for speed; I bought it to make ordinary shopping trips practical.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: Most of my cycling is on woodland trails near where I live. They're great fun, but in winter there are muddy areas, stones and tree roots everywhere. The light city bike I borrowed from my sister clearly wasn't designed for that. I selected a mountain bike with wide tyres and strong suspension because it can handle rough paths without slipping or making every bump painful. It's less convenient in town, but on the routes I actually enjoy riding, it has made an enormous difference.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: My aim this year is to cycle to the coast, which is over sixty kilometres from home. The bike I had at school was fine for short rides, but my shoulders ached after half an hour on it. At the shop I tried several models and chose the one with a riding position and seat that felt comfortable enough for spending several hours on the road. I paid slightly more than intended, but during training rides I’ve had no discomfort, so it was worthwhile.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I was saving for a bicycle to use at university, although I expected to wait until summer before I had enough money. Then a sports shop near us announced it was closing. The model I wanted was being sold at almost half its normal price in the final sale, so I bought it immediately rather than risk losing such a good bargain. The colour wouldn't have been my first choice, but when something costs that much less, I can live with bright orange.

options.text: It was suitable for carrying things.
It was easy to store at home.
It could cope with difficult ground.
It was comfortable for long journeys.
It had been recommended by a relative.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The folding bicycle can be carried upstairs and stored in the speaker's small flat (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker needed a bicycle that could carry groceries safely (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker required a bike designed for rough, uneven woodland routes (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker prioritised comfort because he plans to cycle long distances (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The reduced price in a closing-down sale persuaded the speaker to purchase the bicycle (evidence [23]).
